item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I came here expecting to be told that memetic scips don't work that way, but I was pleasantly surprised by a good, thorough and very valid critique of my article. I see where you're coming from, and I can't really refute any of the points you make; you're on the money says my 20-20 hindsight. So thanks I guess. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "'murica fuck yea" aspect fell completely flat for me. From a technical perspective, the article spends far more time detailing the transmission and stages of SCP-1681, with the "joke" being in the last two paragraphs, and not being particularly funny. There's also the issue of basic coherence - it's a meme that is incredibly virulent that causes people to babel weird patriotic American platitudes, infecting other people, then they jump off a building and explode into Mickey Mouse memorabilia.

My main problem with this, though, is that it doesn't feel realistic. To be clear, I don't mean in the sense of "umm, people cannot actually explode into bald eagles. ding." Rather, this doesn't seem like something that makes sense for the GRU-P to do in universe. This anomaly comes across as something cooked up a bored, half-interested wizard who never learned anything about the US beyond what they vaguely remembered from TV sitcoms and dirty jokes, instead of the work of an organization trying to ensure Soviet hegemony or even destroy the US, .

Like Comrade Detective (set in communist Romania), the entire gag of this is just "hey, people in the eastern bloc sure had stereotypical views of Americans," as if this was a profound or particularly novel statement. I feel like in both works, there's an interesting story about the ways in which leaning into stereotypes through the use of irony allows the audience to get enjoyment of them. However, I'm not voting on some potential statement but rather what I just read, so I have to give this a downvote.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source found indicates the image was released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: Извлеките средства производства
item.content:encoded:
Excuse the shitty Google translated Russian. I love this piece, it's just absurd. It feels like something a country like the Soviet Union's secret scientists would try and do to help that anti-American sentiment they needed. Or something.

+☭

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show edits
- Hide

To link to mentioned GOIs, I propose the following edits:

despite joint Foundation and Global Occult Coalition efforts to eradicate it outside of containment.

to

despite joint Foundation and Global Occult Coalition efforts to eradicate it outside of containment.

and

Documentation seized from GRU Division "P" archives after its dissolution shows that SCP-1681 was developed by that organization in an effort to influence and control public opinion on the United States.

to
Documentation seized from GRU Division "P" archives after its dissolution shows that SCP-1681 was developed by that organization in an effort to influence and control public opinion on the United States.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even more Murrican then a desert eagle that shoots out a bald eagle that attacks people with Communist beliefs, Russian ancestry, or unpatriotic leanings?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was the most Murrican SCP I've ever saw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'involuntary ingestion of Haliaeetus leucocephalus.'

Welp, that sold me on the upvote xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to think this Skip is everything Michael Bay's dreams are made of.

'MERICA SPLOSION!

+1.776 because FREEDOM!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…
What.
Now I'm imagining a person exploding into a shower of Big Macs travelling at 500m/s. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't going to upvote, but then I looked up the common name of Must Muscullus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Totally.

Yeah, no. Thanks for pointing that out. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it intentional that the addendum has it happening before it was first said to be deployed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had to read this a couple of times before it stopped being too weird to properly place.

Aside from that, I like it. It's bizarre in a wonderfully creative way and has plenty of delicious backstory. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Victims of the US' violent anticommunistic propaganda.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the exploding-into-objects jumpers were supposed to be viewed as terrorists? Or as victims of terrorist attacks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's similar to what they intended, but the results were far more deadly than they'd predicted. What they wanted was something that would link American iconography and terrorism, thereby equating the US to terrorists in the eyes of their own people. What they got was a total clusterfuck of an nigh-unstoppable memetic threat that killed far more people than they intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The suicide-via-exploding-into-high-velocity-animals thing: was that a completely unintended side-effect of the memetic agent, or is it similar to what they intended, but they didn't get it quite right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They taste like chicken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You all have Scantron to thank for the term 'Berliner event'; if he hadn't come up with that, it'd been [SOMETHING?] events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: BOOM?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ich ben ein cheeseburger.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was neutral about this right up until the involuntary ingestion of bald eagles.
I cannot stop snickering.
TAKE IT. TAKE MY UPVOTE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This confused me in the oddest way, which is why I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, you could make a hazmat suit designed to fit an eagle. It's just that there's been no reason to… until now, that is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't it be more like a bunch of eagles wearing a hazmat suit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I…I…feel the need to write another scip now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really? At first glance I thought he meant eagles wearing hazmat suits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking American explosions, I don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was really good up until the end…

in at least one case, from involuntary ingestion of Haliaeetus leucocephalus.

And that just cemented it even more. I think you did a great job of conveying the meaning behind what a Berliner even is without specifically spelling it out.

+1 and MRKA F'K YEH.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was wavering between a downvote and a novote, but then the ending with the explosion of bald eagles was just too damn good.

+1 for MURICA

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Glad to be of help. :)

Overall, it's really well done and has a healthy dose of surrealism that I like to see in skips. Keep it up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haliaeetus leucocephalus should probably be qualified with its common name (bald eagle). Peer-reviewed scientific documents do this in real life, because even Real Scientists™ can't be arsed to Google these things.

Now see, I want people to go 'What the hell is that?' and Google it. You're also the umpteenth person to remark on this, so I'll relent. ;)

Bald eagles are not small birds. How does one accidentally ingest a bald eagle? (Admittedly, I may not want to know the answer to this.)

That's exactly what I'm going for. I'm not entirely sure either, but it's bound to be messy.

MTF should probably be spelled out as Mobile Task Force the first time it's used in a document, just for clinicality's sake.

Noted.

MTF Chi-3 is one of the example task forces in Task Forces. While you're certainly not obligated to change this, I did spend a lot of time making sure that nothing on that guide clashed with existing usage. :)

Dammit. I always check whether I'm going to do something that conflicts with established canon. I'll edit.

A significant amount of time was spent trying to ensure that the various nicknames for the example task forces were suitably conversational, meaning that it's reasonably non-silly to say things like, "we need to call in the Exorcists" or "it's already being handled by the Debuggers". "Mops and Buckets" doesn't lend itself to casual usage very well.

Aww…I like "Mop and Bucket". :( Will edit though, in light of the next point.

Very minor personal point as well, it seems really strange for two separate Mobile Task Forces to be mobilized for a single SCP; what's the point of a specialized task force if it can only monitor or handle a situation, but not both? Another option is that Foundation field agents across the world monitor for emergence, but a task force is responsible for handling it.

You're probably right.

"auditory memetic cognitohazard" is a bit weird, since "memetic agent" already implies "cognitohazard". By common usage, "auditory memetic agent" works just fine.

Motherfucker, I knew it. This is my first attempt at a memetic threat and I listened to someone who told me "auditory memetic agent" wasn't enough. :|

All memetic agents are self-propagating. Did you mean "spontaneous outbreak" instead?

Yup, you got me.

All in all, good points. Thanks, Aelanna. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of minor sticking points:

  • Haliaeetus leucocephalus should probably be qualified with its common name (bald eagle). Peer-reviewed scientific documents do this in real life, because even Real Scientists™ can't be arsed to Google these things.
  • Bald eagles are not small birds. How does one accidentally ingest a bald eagle? (Admittedly, I may not want to know the answer to this.)
  • MTF should probably be spelled out as Mobile Task Force the first time it's used in a document, just for clinicality's sake.
  • MTF Chi-3 is one of the example task forces in Task Forces. While you're certainly not obligated to change this, I did spend a lot of time making sure that nothing on that guide clashed with existing usage. :)
  • A significant amount of time was spent trying to ensure that the various nicknames for the example task forces were suitably conversational, meaning that it's reasonably non-silly to say things like, "we need to call in the Exorcists" or "it's already being handled by the Debuggers". "Mops and Buckets" doesn't lend itself to casual usage very well.
  • Very minor personal point as well, it seems really strange for two separate Mobile Task Forces to be mobilized for a single SCP; what's the point of a specialized task force if it can only monitor or handle a situation, but not both? Another option is that Foundation field agents across the world monitor for emergence, but a task force is responsible for handling it.
  • "auditory memetic cognitohazard" is a bit weird, since "memetic agent" already implies "cognitohazard". By common usage, "auditory memetic agent" works just fine.
  • All memetic agents are self-propagating. Did you mean "spontaneous outbreak" instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done. Creative idea and good execution are the recipe for a good SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed that, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
5m of amnesitics in the area around the skip seems too small.

+1 either way

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uhhh…cause I loves me some Hazmat suits?

But seriously, that was left in from a previous version that had people exploding into material associated with environmental disasters. And then Hazmat becomes common sense.

Let me get that for you.

EDIT: That is a hilarious visual, by the way, guys in Hazmat suits trying to capture bald eagles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why hazmat suits for the recovery teams? Not only does the infection not spread through any biologically hazardous media, but…have you ever tried to catch bald eagles wearing a hazmat suit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As for the writing: Oops, I did it again. Seriously, if there's one consistent flaw with my writing, it's that I go too formal. Will edit, thanks.

As for what you're supposed to get from this: it's an attempt to weaponize memetics and influence public opinion by associating acts of terrorism with larger-than-life iconography of the enemy. While not subtle, and definitely flawed (I mean, the fuckers lost the damn key to stopping this thing), it would probably be effective circa 1982. And yes, there's also absurdity and the humorous aftertaste, but this isn't meant to be just funny.

I do appreciate your comments though. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is actually how I pictured GRU-P work to be.

A hell of a clean-up.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
mmmmrm

This is a neutral vote on my part. There are some wonky areas where the formality of the writing overtakes the functionality (" until such a time as a Berliner event spontaneously occurs," "an SCP-1681-1 instance (one Boleslava Medvedeva)"). In regards to the idea itself, I'm…bluh, honestly, I really have no idea what I'm supposed to be getting out of this. Is it supposed to be funny? Should I be laughing? Is it, like, scary? People bursting into bald eagles is not scary. It's absurd, and I get the feeling that this whole thing is supposed to be really absurd and that's why I should upvote, but there's nothing really interesting behind the absurdity. Like, the only real thing I can see behind the absurdity is the stereotypical "capitalist pigdog" strain of humor. Which doesn't particularly make me want to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I'll surely need to reread this after some extensive thinking but it's still an upvote form me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit Scantron, now I can't credit you in the first post.

Anyway, thanks to VAELynx, Echo, Faustov, Scantron and coffeefueled for their assistance in developing this scip from shit to shine. If I've missed anyone, feel free to rant at me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
inb4 'murrika
inb4 freedom
inb4 SCP-50-AE-J
inb4 upvoters can't inb4
+1
